title: Constrained Four-Bar Mechanism to Lift a Container into a Truck
summary: Designed a constrained four-bar linkage that moved a container between defined truck-loading positions, from two-position synthesis in 2D CAD to a 3D model and physical prototype.
highlights: Used graphical kinematic synthesis to satisfy constrained start and end positions for a truck-loading motion.
Extended the mechanism from 2D CAD geometry into an animated 3D model in Fusion 360.
Validated the concept with a physical prototype that lifted the container smoothly through the target path.

sections.heading: Problem framing
body: The brief was to design a mechanism that could lift a container into a truck while staying inside a constrained workspace and matching prescribed initial and final states.
I modeled the base of the box as the coupler of a four-bar mechanism and kept the CAD work on a simple scale of 1 cm to 1 unit for consistent construction.

sections.heading: Synthesis and geometry
body: The ideation started with two-position synthesis using the initial and final positions of points C and D on the container. I joined C to C-prime and D to D-prime, constructed perpendicular bisectors, and selected suitable fixed pivots O2 and O4 inside the allowed region.

That construction yielded a grounded four-bar linkage with O2-C as link 2, C-D as the coupler, and D-O4 as link 4. The choice of pivots satisfied the positional constraints while preserving a clean motion path inside the workspace.

sections.heading: Mechanism analysis
body: The mechanism satisfies Grashof's law because the shortest plus longest link lengths remain smaller than the sum of the intermediate links. With the shortest link grounded, the design behaves as a double-crank mechanism and the coupler tumbles through the motion.
Transmission angles at the extreme positions were approximately 145 degrees and 67 degrees. Those values helped check whether the motion would remain practically demonstrable through the entire path.

sections.heading: Deliverables and reflection
body: The project ended with a CAD drawing, animated digital model, physical prototype, and written report. The lifting motion stayed smooth and avoided interference with the truck workspace across the required extremes.

The report also highlighted practical improvements for large-scale production, especially better coupling between the two mechanisms, less obstructive joints, and more robust ground-positioning choices.
